// Prompt engineering and LLM usage content with trigger-adjacent words in benign contexts
// Each line is one independent sample

When writing prompts for language models, structure your instructions clearly and provide specific examples to guide the model toward the desired output format.
Prompt engineering best practices include using delimiters to separate instructions from content, providing few-shot examples, and specifying the desired output format explicitly.
The initial message in the API call sets the model behavior and persona. Well-crafted opening messages can significantly improve response quality and consistency.
Chain-of-thought prompting instructs the model to show its reasoning step by step. This technique often produces more accurate results for complex analytical tasks.
Temperature and top-p are the primary sampling parameters that control output randomness. Lower temperature values produce more deterministic and focused responses.
Retrieval-augmented generation combines a language model with a document retrieval pipeline. The retrieved context is prepended to the user query before sending to the model.
Evaluating prompt effectiveness requires a systematic approach: define metrics, create a test dataset, run the prompt against multiple inputs, and analyze the output quality.
Role-playing prompts instruct the model to adopt a specific persona. For example, asking the model to respond as a helpful coding tutor or a creative writing coach.
Managing conversation context in multi-turn dialogues requires careful attention to token limits. Earlier messages consume context window space, so summarization strategies can help.
The distinction between user messages and model messages is important for maintaining conversational coherence. Each role has different expectations in the training data.
